annotations.label: Combinatorial game theory

anchor.snippet: Combinatorial game theory is a branch of mathematics and theoretical computer science

annotations.note: No general definition of combinatorial game theory is present in Mathlib4; the only related file is `Mathlib/Order/GameAdd.lean`, which defines an abstract game-addition relation.

annotations.label: Solved game

anchor.snippet: A key concept in combinatorial game theory is that of the solved game

annotations.note: No notion of `SolvedGame` or analogous predicate appears in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Tic-tac-toe is solved (draw)

anchor.snippet: tic-tac-toe is solved in that optimal play by both participants always results in a draw

annotations.note: Tic-tac-toe is not formalized in Mathlib4 (no occurrences of `TicTacToe`).

annotations.label: Checkers weakly solved (2007)

anchor.snippet: in 2007, checkers was announced to be weakly solved

annotations.note: Checkers is not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Mathgames vs playgames

anchor.snippet: It is useful to distinguish between combinatorial "mathgames"

annotations.note: No such distinction is formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Impartial game

anchor.snippet: the theory of impartial games , in which any play available to one player must be available to the other as well

annotations.note: No definition of impartial game (e.g. `PGame.Impartial`) is present in current Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Normal play condition

anchor.snippet: subject to the normal play condition , which means that a player who cannot move loses

annotations.note: Normal play convention is not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Sprague–Grundy theorem (impartial games)

anchor.snippet: the Sprague–Grundy theorem showed that all impartial games are equivalent to heaps in Nim

annotations.note: Grep for `Sprague` returns no hits in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Partisan game

anchor.snippet: jointly introduced the theory of a partisan game , in which the requirement that a play available to one player be available to both is relaxed

annotations.note: No definition of partisan game is present in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Sum of two games

anchor.snippet: the sum of two games, which is a game where each player may choose to move either in one game or the other at any point in the game

annotations.note: `Prod.GameAdd` (and `Sym2.GameAdd`) in `Mathlib/Order/GameAdd.lean` model the move relation for the sum of two games abstractly, but no `PGame`-based sum operation is present.

annotations.label: Blue–Red Hackenbush

anchor.snippet: Blue–Red Hackenbush - At the finite level, this partisan combinatorial game allows constructions of games whose values are dyadic rational numbers

annotations.note: Hackenbush is not formalized in Mathlib4 (no grep hits).

annotations.label: Blue–Red–Green Hackenbush

anchor.snippet: Blue–Red–Green Hackenbush - Allows for additional game values that are not numbers in the traditional sense

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Toads and Frogs

anchor.snippet: Toads and Frogs - Allows various game values

annotations.note: Toads and Frogs is not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Domineering

anchor.snippet: Domineering - Various interesting games, such as hot games , appear as positions in Domineering

annotations.note: Grep for `Domineering` returns no hits in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Nim

anchor.snippet: Nim - An impartial game

annotations.note: There is no `PGame.nim` or `Nim` game in Mathlib4 (the lone `Nim` grep hit is unrelated text in `Stonean/EffectiveEpi.lean`).

annotations.label: Shannon number (chess complexity)

anchor.snippet: Claude Shannon estimated the lower bound of the game-tree complexity of chess to be

annotations.note: Chess and the Shannon number are not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Game notation {L|R}

anchor.snippet: each game has the notation

annotations.note: No `{L | R}` game notation / `PGame.mk` is currently in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Domineering initial position

anchor.snippet: Using Domineering as an example, label each of the sixteen boxes of the four-by-four board

annotations.note: Domineering is not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Zero game

anchor.snippet: is called the zero game , and can actually be abbreviated 0

annotations.note: No `PGame.zero` / zero game is present in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Star game

anchor.snippet: it is called the star game , which can also be abbreviated

annotations.note: No `PGame.star` / star game is present in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Numbers (move advantage)

anchor.snippet: Numbers represent the number of free moves, or the move advantage of a particular player

annotations.note: Game numbers are not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Zero game is a first-player loss

anchor.snippet: The zero game is a loss for the first player

annotations.note: Not formalized; depends on a `PGame` framework absent from Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Sum of number games behaves like integers

anchor.snippet: The sum of number games behaves like the integers

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Game numbers are surreal numbers

anchor.snippet: Any game number is in the class of the surreal numbers

annotations.note: No `Surreal` type appears in Mathlib4 (no `Surreal*.lean` files).

annotations.label: Star ∗

anchor.snippet: Star , written as ∗ or {0|0}, is a first-player win

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib4.

anchor.snippet: because the first player must turn one copy of ∗ to a 0

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Fuzzy / confused with 0

anchor.snippet: are said to be fuzzy or confused with 0

annotations.note: No `Fuzzy` / `‖` relation on games is in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: ∗n (nimber notation)

anchor.snippet: The game ∗n is notation for

annotations.note: Nimber notation `∗n` is not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Up and down

anchor.snippet: Up , written as ↑, is a position in combinatorial game theory

annotations.note: The games `↑`/`↓` are not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Up positive, down negative, both infinitesimal

anchor.snippet: Up is strictly positive (↑ > 0), and down is strictly negative

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Hot game / switch ±n

anchor.snippet: Both moves in this game are an advantage for the player who makes them; so the game is said to be "hot;"

annotations.note: Hot games and switches are not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Impartial game

anchor.snippet: An impartial game is one where, at every position of the game, the same moves are available to both players

annotations.note: No `Impartial` predicate on games is present in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Nimbers

anchor.snippet: the games defined in this way are known as nimbers

annotations.note: Grep for `nimber` returns no hits in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Sprague–Grundy theorem

anchor.snippet: The Sprague–Grundy theorem states that every impartial game under the normal play convention is equivalent to a nimber

annotations.note: Not formalized; no Sprague–Grundy declaration exists in current Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Smallest nimbers are 0 and ∗

anchor.snippet: The "smallest" nimbers – the simplest and least under the usual ordering of the ordinals – are 0 and ∗

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib4.
